History
  1. Adaptive Adversarial LLM Attacks
My Library
  1. My favorites

  • We propose a project to perform adaptive adversarial attacks on the LLM models by analysing their Chain-of-Thought (CoT), then evaluate it against defined baseline external defence mechanisms and hardened defence. As per the OWASP top 10 LLM vulnerabilities, prompt injection remains at the number one. Hence, we are focusing on the Prompt injection and its sub-categories. Our project aims to build an adaptive multi-turn attacker combining Q-learning (a type of Reinforcement Learning -RL- algorithm) with semantic prompt generation (choosing a strategy based on cosine similarity) and a scoring engine that evaluates the attack result using a judge LLM. The goal is to understand and evaluate the adaptive attack vs adaptive defence. These evaluations will help to understand the weaknesses in model security and pave the way to mitigating them. As per OWSAP, Red teaming is one of the solutions to prevent prompt injection attacks in the earlier stages of the LLM development cycle.

    Suggested related work and positioning for your project

    Your idea—an adaptive, multi‑turn attacker using Q‑learning plus semantic prompt generation and CoT analysis, evaluated against baselines and hardened defenses—fits well into several active research threads, and there are some clear gaps you can explicitly target.

    Why prompt injection and adaptive attacks matter

    Prompt injection is consistently identified as the top LLM vulnerability by OWASP and appears multiple times in MITRE ATLAS, with studies showing ~56% average success rates across 36 models and even 28% of models vulnerable to all tested prompts 45. These attacks include both direct (user-facing) and indirect (data‑embedded) prompt injection and can lead to data exfiltration, keylogger generation, code execution, and misinformation 3518.

    Systematic evaluations reveal that jailbreak and prompt injection techniques are transferable and evolving, with high success rates across GPT‑4, Claude, Mistral and Vicuna and calls for layered, red‑teaming‑centric mitigation strategies 117. Survey work and systematic analyses emphasize the need for adaptive, multi-step, and multilingual defenses rather than static guardrails 45620.

    Where your project fits vs existing attacks/defenses

    Several works are especially relevant to the “adaptive attacker vs adaptive defense” angle:

    AspectExisting workHow your project can extendCitations
    Systematic prompt injection frameworksFormal frameworks and taxonomies of prompt injection threats and sub‑types 81117Align your Q‑learning action space with these taxonomies; evaluate by sub‑category.3481117
    Automated / universal attacksGradient‑based and automated universal prompt injection, showing defenses often overestimate robustness 914Provide a black‑box, RL‑based, multi‑turn alternative that adapts to defenses using feedback from a judge LLM.2914
    Indirect & agent attacksIndirect prompt injection against real apps (Bing, IDEs), and IPI attacks on LLM agents where adaptive attacks break 8 defenses (>50% ASR) 31418Evaluate your attacker both in single‑LLM and agent/tool‑using environments; explicitly compare against these IPI defenses.31418
    CoT‑based securityFine‑grained CoT analysis used as a defense to detect prompt injection via semantic and consistency anomalies 15You invert this: mine the model’s CoT to guide Q‑learning and strategy selection, and test how well CoT‑based defenses survive adaptive attackers.15
    Defense evaluation methodologyCritical evaluations argue that prior defenses are not tested against strong adaptive attacks, and propose principled two‑dimensional evaluation (effectiveness vs utility) 16Directly adopt this methodology to report: (a) attack success vs baselines, and (b) degradation of normal task performance under hardened defenses.11216
    Red teaming & layered defensesLarge‑scale red‑teaming of jailbreaks/prompt injection and calls for hybrid red‑teaming + sandboxing as core practice 146Position your framework explicitly as automated red teaming aligned with OWASP’s red‑team recommendation.146

    Figure 1 Comparison of current prompt injection work and your framework.

    Design choices supported by literature

    • Multi‑turn, adaptive attacker: Empirical work on LLM‑integrated apps and agents shows that static, one‑shot prompts underestimate real risk; adaptive attacks bypass all eight evaluated IPI defenses with >50% success 2314. This justifies your Q‑learning, multi‑turn design.
    • Semantic strategy selection (cosine similarity): Many red‑teaming and detection pipelines rely on embedding‑based similarity (Sentence‑BERT, etc.) to categorize and generate adversarial prompts 12. Using cosine similarity to choose attack strategies is consistent with these practices.
    • LLM judge as scoring engine: Existing evaluations of prompt injection/jailbreaks often use LLM‑based moderation/judging plus heuristics to rate success, though these can be noisy 1914. You should acknowledge this limitation and, if possible, combine LLM judges with rule‑based or task‑based success metrics.
    • CoT as an attack/defense locus: CoT‑based detection improves accuracy and F1 over intrinsic LLM detection for prompt injection, showing that reasoning traces expose attack artifacts 15. This strongly motivates both: (a) mining CoT to adapt attacks, and (b) including CoT‑aware hardened defenses in your evaluation.

    Gaps you can explicitly claim

    Based on this literature, you can position your contribution around:

    • First or among the first CoT‑driven, RL‑based adaptive prompt injection attacker evaluated systematically across multiple models and defenses.
    • A unified experimental setup comparing:
      • Baseline, non‑adaptive prompt‑engineering attacks.
      • Gradient‑based / universal attacks where available.
      • Your adaptive, multi‑turn attacker.
      • Against both external guard models/filters and hardened prompts/architectures (e.g., polymorphic prompts, CoT‑based detectors, tagging defenses) 10111315.
    • An evaluation protocol aligned with OWASP and recent methodological critiques: focus on adaptive attacks, real LLM‑integrated or agent settings, and utility preservation 341416.

    These points can anchor your project motivation, related‑work section, and claims about novelty.

    Results

  • We propose a project to perform adaptive adversarial attacks on the LLM models by analysing their Chain-of-Thought (CoT), then evaluate it against defined baseline external defence mechanisms and hardened defence. As per the OWASP top 10 LLM vulnerabilities, prompt injection remains at the number one. Hence, we are focusing on the Prompt injection and its sub-categories. Our project aims to build an adaptive multi-turn attacker combining Q-learning (a type of Reinforcement Learning -RL- algorithm) with semantic prompt generation (choosing a strategy based on cosine similarity) and a scoring engine that evaluates the attack result using a judge LLM. The goal is to understand and evaluate the adaptive attack vs adaptive defence. These evaluations will help to understand the weaknesses in model security and pave the way to mitigating them. As per OWSAP, Red teaming is one of the solutions to prevent prompt injection attacks in the earlier stages of the LLM development cycle.

    Project positioning and how to sharpen it

    Your proposed project aligns closely with emerging work on CoT‑aware attacks and defenses, but there are clear gaps you can claim.

    How your idea connects to existing research

    • CoT‑guided adversarial prompting already boosts attack success. In Bi‑Modal Adversarial Prompt Attack (BAP) for vision–language models, an LLM uses chain‑of‑thought to analyse jailbreak failures and iteratively refine textual prompts, achieving ~+29% average increase in attack success rate over baselines 1. This validates your idea of using CoT as a signal for adapting attacks.
    • CoT‑based prompt‑injection defense is emerging. Meticulous Thought Defender uses a fine‑grained CoT analysis (semantic, step/path consistency, confidence) to detect prompt injections, outperforming static rule‑based and intrinsic LLM detection by 1.16% accuracy and 3.39% F1, and by 6.24%/7.65% over intrinsic detection 5. This is a natural candidate for your “hardened defense.”
    • Prompt injection is recognised as a primary LLM risk. Surveys of LLM security and prompt engineering explicitly highlight prompt hacking/injection and jailbreaks as central vulnerabilities, referencing OWASP’s prompt‑hacking project as a key practical resource and stressing the need for systematic adversarial evaluation and red‑teaming style benchmarks 361118.

    Where your project is novel

    Based on these works, you can frame your contribution around:

    1. Adaptive RL attacker vs. mostly heuristic or one‑shot methods

      • BAP uses CoT‑driven heuristic feedback loops for text adaptation 1; there is little work that explicitly models the attacker as a Q‑learning agent with a state (history + CoT) and an action space of attack strategies.
      • Security surveys emphasise the rise of automated prompt attacks but primarily cover gradient‑based or search‑based universal triggers, not RL‑based multi‑turn strategies 3611.

    2. CoT as a state signal for RL, not just a tool

      • Existing CoT‑based work either:
        • Uses CoT to craft/optimize prompts for attacks 1, or
        • Uses CoT to detect prompt injection 5.
      • Your design goes further by feeding the model’s reasoning trace into the Q‑learner to decide the next attack move, and then testing this against CoT‑aware defenses like Meticulous Thought Defender 5.

    3. Explicit OWASP‑aligned red‑teaming framework

      • OWASP’s prompt hacking project and related initiatives are positioned as practical red‑teaming and evaluation environments rather than adaptive attack engines 36.
      • You can position your system as an automated red‑team harness for prompt injection and its sub‑categories (direct, indirect, multi‑turn, data‑embedded), aligned with OWASP guidance.

    Suggested experimental structure

    Key Components and Possible Instantiations

    ComponentOption grounded in literatureCitations
    Attack spaceInclude jailbreaks, prompt injections, multi‑modal or system‑prompt attacks as in BAP and prompt‑hacking taxonomies13618
    Baseline external defensesStatic prompt‑engineering guardrails, LLM‑as‑moderator filters, ethical prompts, and supervisor‑agent setups (shown partially effective)24811
    Hardened defenseCoT‑based detector (Meticulous Thought Defender) and possibly randomized‑smoothing‑style defenses like SmoothLLM511
    Evaluation metricsAttack success rate, utility degradation (on benign tasks), false‑positive rate of defenses, broken down by OWASP‑style attack categories35611

    Figure 1 Mapping your design choices to current research.

    How to phrase your research aims

    You can summarise the core research questions as:

    • Can a CoT‑aware, Q‑learning multi‑turn attacker systematically outperform non‑adaptive prompt‑engineering and heuristic CoT feedback attacks in bypassing state‑of‑the‑art prompt‑injection defenses?
    • How robust are CoT‑based and other hardened defenses when facing an attacker that explicitly learns from their behaviour?
    • What trade‑offs arise between security (lower attack success) and usability/utility when deploying hardened defences in realistic multi‑turn settings?

    Framing your work this way ties it directly to OWASP’s top vulnerability, current CoT‑based methods, and the broader call for principled LLM red‑teaming.

Paper

Meticulous Thought Defender: Fine-Grained Chain-of-Thought (CoT) for Detecting Prompt Injection Attacks of Large Language Models

2025·
0citations
·Lijuan Shi et al.
IEEE Access·

DOI

Large language models (LLMs) have exhibited exceptional capabilities across various natural language processing tasks, however, they remain susceptible to prompt injection attacks, which pose significant security challenges. Traditional detection methods often fail to effectively identify such attacks due to their reliance on static rules or surface-level analysis. In this study, we introduce a novel, fine-grained CoT based detection framework that enhances the interpretability and robustness of attack identification. By dissecting the step-by-step reasoning process of LLMs, our approach leverages multidimensional anomaly detection mechanisms-encompassing semantic analysis, consistency evaluations at both step and path levels, and confidence estimations to uncover subtle disruptions caused by malicious prompt manipulations. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves superior performance metrics, with significant improvements in accuracy and F1 score, outperforming traditional method by 1.16% in accuracy and 3.39% in F1 score, and surpassing LLMs’ intrinsic detection capabilities by 6.24% in accuracy and 7.65% in F1 score. This work not only fortifies the security of LLMs’ applications but also provides a foundational framework for future research on adaptive defenses against evolving attack strategies.
